metadata.content: I remember sitting in my dorm freshman year, scrolling through LinkedIn and it felt like everyone already knew what they were doing. People were talking about internships, networking, referrals, and I didn't even know what those words really meant. My parents didn't go to college, so I didn't have anyone to ask. I thought I was just behind, like I had already somehow failed. Sophomore year, I almost didn't apply to any internships because I assumed I wasn't qualified, but one night I randomly went to a career workshop, mostly for the free food, but that's where I learned that no one actually expects you to know everything. You're kind of supposed to figure it out as you go. I applied to 27 internships and got rejected from 26, but that one yes changed everything. What I wish I knew earlier is this: Everyone looks like they have a plan, but most people are just guessing confidently. Don't count yourself out before you even try.
metadata.summary: A first-generation college student overcomes feelings of inadequacy and a lack of professional guidance by attending a career workshop and persevering through numerous rejections. They eventually secure an internship, learning that success comes from trying despite uncertainty and realizing that many peers are also figuring things out as they go.

metadata.content: As a first-generation student, and the only one of my family to make it to college, I wish I knew how heavy the pressure would feel. My parents came to this country at my age without knowing the language, and I carry that with me every day. It makes me feel like I can't fail because they sacrificed so much for me to have opportunities they didn't. The pressure can be very hard sometimes, but I've learned to use it to push myself and thrive instead of letting it break me. I remind myself that their journey and mine are different, and I don't have to be perfect. I would tell other first-gen students that it's okay to feel the pressure, but don't let it consume you and use it as a way to motivate you.
metadata.summary: A first-generation student reflects on the heavy pressure of carrying their family's sacrifices and the fear of failure that comes with being the first to attend college. They eventually learned to transform this pressure into motivation while accepting that they do not have to be perfect. The story encourages other students to acknowledge their feelings without letting them become overwhelming.

metadata.summary: A first-generation college student shares the heavy emotional weight of succeeding to honor their immigrant parents' sacrifices. By reframing this pressure as motivation rather than a demand for perfection, they have learned to thrive and encourage others to do the same.

metadata.content: I remember sitting in my dorm freshman year, scrolling through LinkedIn, and it felt like everyone already knew what they were doing. People were talking about internships, networking, referrals, and I didn't even know what those words really meant. My parents didn't go to college, so I didn't have anyone to ask. I thought I was just behind, like I had already somehow failed. Sophomore year, I almost didn't apply to any internships because I assumed I wasn't qualified, but one night I randomly went to a career workshop, mostly for the free food, but that's where I learned that no one actually expects you to know everything. You're kind of supposed to figure it out as you go. I applied to 27 internships and got rejected from 26, but that one "yes" changed everything. metadata.summary: A first-generation student initially felt overwhelmed and behind their peers who seemed to naturally understand the professional world of internships and networking. After attending a career workshop and overcoming the fear of rejection, they applied to dozens of roles and secured a life-changing opportunity. Their journey taught them that success often comes from persevering despite uncertainty and not counting oneself out.
